feedingProfile.preferredNpk: balanced low-strength (e.g. 5-5-5); herbs need low fertility for best leaf flavor

commonIssues: Powdery mildew in humid or crowded conditions; cut affected stems back hard to stimulate fresh growth (RHS, Wisconsin Extension).
Susceptible to root rot if overwatered or left in waterlogged soil, especially over cold wet winters (Old Farmer's Almanac, RHS).
Spreads aggressively by self-seeding and runners; grow in a container to curtail spread and deadhead after flowering (RHS, Wisconsin Extension).
Occasional leaf spot, gray mold, and leafhoppers (Missouri Botanical Garden, RHS).
Container plants benefit from a sheltered spot over winter out of excessive rain (RHS).

source.citation: University of Wisconsin-Madison Division of Extension - Wisconsin Horticulture: Lemon Balm, Melissa officinalis. Full sun in fertile well-drained soil; hardy zones 3a-9b; plants ~2 ft tall; space 16-18 in apart; few pest/disease problems, occasional powdery mildew; sow indoors 4-6 weeks before last frost.
University of Wisconsin-Madison Division of Extension - Wisconsin Horticulture: Lemon Balm. Hardy zones 3a to 9b (perennial); planting/start timing and spacing.

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder: Melissa officinalis. Full sun to part shade; dry to medium well-drained soils, avoid wet/winter moisture; USDA zones 3-7 (Plant Finder); height 1.5-2 ft, spread 1.5-3 ft; low maintenance; occasional powdery mildew, leaf spot, gray mold; bloom June-August.
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder: Melissa officinalis. Light = full sun to part shade, afternoon shade beneficial in hot climates; bloom June-August informs productive months.

Royal Horticultural Society (RHS): How to grow Lemon balm. Sun or partial shade; container plants kept evenly moist while established garden plants are drought-tolerant; grow in large pots at least 25cm (10in) wide and deep in peat-free soil-based compost; tolerates most soils but avoid waterlogging; powdery mildew and leafhoppers occasional; container plants moved to shelter over winter.

Royal Horticultural Society (RHS): How to grow Lemon balm. Container plants need regular moisture to keep compost evenly moist; established garden plants are drought-tolerant - informs medium drying tolerance and overwatering/root-rot caution.

The Old Farmer's Almanac: Lemon Balm Planting, Growing, and Harvesting Guide. Avoid overwatering - lemon balm is sensitive to root rot; needs moist (not oversaturated) soil; works well in a small pot or patio planter; herbs generally do not need highly fertile soil (very fertile soil reduces leaf flavor); space transplants ~18 in apart.

Confidence note: No authoritative source gives an exact watering interval (days), feeding interval (days), or pot volume in liters for lemon balm. baseIntervalDays=3 and overwaterRisk=high are derived conservatively from RHS 'keep compost evenly moist' for containers combined with Almanac/MoBot root-rot and dry-to-medium guidance. feedingProfile.baseIntervalDays=30 is a conservative herb default consistent with Almanac's 'herbs do not need highly fertile soil.' containerProfile liters are converted from RHS 'at least 25cm (10in) wide and deep' (a ~25cm pot approximates 11-12 L); idealMax estimated. hotWeatherMultiplier, rainSkipThresholdMm, coldHardyMinC (zone 3 lower bound ~ -34C) and heatStressC (~30C, afternoon-shade benefit) are reasonable estimates, not directly stated.
